data.title: Q2 Holdings: Trim This Stock As Value Fades (Downgrade)
data.description:
While the stock market has rebounded sharply from April lows as Wall Street gears up for a flurry of new trade deals, we shouldn't get overly complacent. I'm still using valuation as the key driver of my buy and sell decisions, and I'm increasing my cash holdings as the stock market heats back up, divesting stocks that, I think, are getting too frothy.

Q2 Holdings (QTWO), in my view, is one of these names. This banking software provider is still down ~10% year to date, but its recent rebound rally (especially after a well-received Q1 earnings print) has helped the company recoup a lot of this year's losses, and the stock is still sitting on ~40% gains over the past year. The key question going forward is: can Q2 continue to justify its valuation multiples, and does it deserve being held in your portfolio?

I last wrote a buy opinion on Q2 Holdings in February, when the stock was trading in the mid-$80s. Since then, Q2 has rebounded modestly (while most SaaS peers have fallen), while the company's recent growth trends are relatively "business as usual" and hardly justify any further multiples expansion. With that in mind, I'm downgrading my viewpoint on Q2 to a neutral rating.

The core issue is that so many of Q2's peers in the software sector remain at decimated levels and offer better value, while Q2 still sits on its vulnerable premiums. At current share prices near $89, Q2 Holdings trades at a market cap of $5.56 billion. After we net off the $486.0 million of cash and $492.9 million of convertible debt on Q2's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $5.57 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Q2 has slightly raised its revenue expectations to $776-$783 million (11-12% y/y growth), barely higher than $772-$779 million previously, and also upped its adjusted EBITDA range by $5 million on both ends to $170-$175 million, implying a 22.1% margin at the midpoint.

This puts Q2's valuation multiples at:

  • 7.2x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 32x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

Here's the problem: so many of Q2's peers now sit below its valuation multiples. Companies like Salesforce (CRM), Workday (WDAY), and Adobe (ADBE) - all at a similar low/mid-teens growth pace - trade at cheaper valuation multiples.

Making the comparison even clunkier for Q2 is the fact that its gross margin profile, currently in the high 50s, sits at a deficit to these SaaS peers (in the mid/high 70s) - a reflection of the company's concentration of lower-margin, non-subscription implementation work, and also meaning the company's revenue stream is less valuable.

All in all, I see limited justification to keep holding on to Q2 Holdings in my portfolio. Take advantage of the recent upside to trim your position and reinvest the proceeds elsewhere.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Q2 Holdings' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Q2's revenue grew 15% y/y to $189.7 million, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $186.5 million (+13% y/y) while also accelerating two points versus Q4's 15% y/y growth rate. We do have to acknowledge the strong performance here in a quarter where many enterprise software companies have reported that macroeconomic conditions have slowed down the deal cycle.

Q2's ARR grew 11% y/y to $847 million, which we note exceeds its guidance for FY25: hopefully an indication that the guidance is still conservative and has room to be raised.

Another indicator of strength: Q2 Holdings' RPO, or remaining performance obligations, also grew 20% y/y to $2.3 billion, adding ~$100 million of net-new backlog within the quarter. As Q2 Holdings doesn't report billings, RPO/backlog growth is one of the most telling forward-looking indicators of the company's growth trajectory.

In particular, Q2 Holdings notes that its anti-fraud detection software tools have seen strong traction among its banking customers. Management notes that the company has a full pipeline of new customers, plus a healthy plate of renewals in Q2 that hopefully lead to more expansion deals. Per CEO Matt Flake's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Looking ahead, I do want to provide a few remarks on our outlook for the remainder of the year. I believe our business is durable due to the mission-critical nature of our products, our strong and diverse customer base, and the fundamentals of our model that have helped us execute well in other recent periods of macro uncertainty. The pandemic, rising rates, and the events in the banking sector from March of ‘23 underscored the importance of deposit gathering, and our solutions are essential in helping customers attract, retain, grow, and protect deposits. We expect that focus on deposits to persist, and it has been a tailwind to our sales efforts over the last several quarters. We have a highly strategic and diverse customer base across our solution areas, and we believe our regional and community financial institution customers play an essential role in supporting the economy, including in challenging times.

In terms of what we see here in early May 2025, we continue to see a solid pipeline for the remainder of the year, with a strong overall renewal and expansion opportunity ahead of us."

Still, we should proceed with caution here. While banks reported strong earnings in Q1 driven by healthy trading desks, most bank executives cautioned on the possibility of tariffs driving us into a recession this year (Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase (JPM), said that a recession is a direct and "likely outcome" of tariffs in April). Since the banking sector is often one of the first sectors to be hit in a recession, it stands to reason that many banks may pull back on their capex and spending plans: hurting new business momentum for Q2 Holdings. In other words, I think Q2's full-year guidance boost may be premature if we start to see banks' procurement trends changing in Q2 and beyond.

Margins remained strong. Q2's adjusted EBITDA grew 62% y/y to $40.7 million, hitting a 21.5% margin: 630bps better y/y.

As a refresher, the company's medium-term operating model calls for a minimum of 360bps of y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion through FY26, driven by economies of scale on sales and marketing spend.

Key takeaways

Though I continue to believe that Q2 Holdings offers solid, consistent near-term performance, I'm worried about the possible impact of a macro recession impacting banks' buying activity and slowing down Q2's recent growth trajectory. On top of this, the stock already sits at a questionable premium to similarly growing software peers, leaving Q2 vulnerable for a near-term correction. Start to reduce your holdings in this stock before it's too late.

data.title: Looming Recession? Here's What BDC Investors Should Look For
data.description:
Introduction

Since 2022, many economists and analysts have been calling for a recession. That's because one quarter that year the economy experienced negative GDP growth. And during the recent report, the U.S. saw another negative quarter.

Will the economy finally see a recession? In my opinion, chances are high. Reason being is higher for longer interest rates are starting to weigh on consumers despite being 100 basis points lower.

Moreover, I'll make a bold prediction that the President actually wants a recession. Trump has been advocating for lower interest rates for some time now, and a recession would likely be a reason the FED would lower them.

With recession talks increasing since the beginning of this year due to tariffs, the BDC sector (BIZD) will likely see some selling pressure. In this article, I discuss why and how high-yield investors should play the sector going forward.

Will We Finally Get A Recession?

By definition, if the next quarter experiences negative growth, then technically we would be in a recession. Would we admit it? Maybe, maybe not. But BDCs could see underperformance in comparison to other sectors.

During downturns or period of high inflation, Healthcare (XLV), Consumer Staples (XLP), Gold (GLD), & REITs (XLRE) often do well.

That's because consumers still need necessary items like healthcare, groceries, and auto retailers. Gold is often seen as a hedge against inflation and performs well in times of uncertainty.

And REITs lease buildings to those tenants that sell necessities like Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), & AutoZone (AZO).

So, they collect stable cash flows from recession-resistant tenants. And with higher yields than average, investors look to the sector for income stability.

Although all companies are subject to headwinds during a recession, larger, investment-rated companies like Home Depot (HD), Starbucks (SBUX), & PepsiCo (PEP) have higher chances of navigating successfully than smaller, non-rated peers.

BDCs also have higher yields, and like REITs, are highly sensitive to the direction of interest rates. And with interest rates expected to be lower with 3 rate cuts anticipated, they could see selling pressure in the coming months.

However, this doesn't mean investors should consider selling. But what you should do is consider downsizing if you own a ton of BDCs.

And instead focus on those with modest leverage, well-staggered debt, and plenty of liquidity to give flexibility should a downturn occur. BDCs lend to smaller, sometimes financially distressed borrowers and act as banks.

If and when a recession occurs, these companies are more susceptible to facing financial difficulty. Lower base rates = lower portfolio yields. Meaning, their bottom lines will likely decline, forcing tight dividend coverage.

For example, one BDC I warned investors about that previously that recently cut the dividend is Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL). The BDC cut its dividend from $0.55 to $0.40 as a result of declining financials.

Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) was also forced to cut their dividend by 29% from $0.45 to $0.32. Management stated the change to their dividend policy provides flexibility, but their double-digit decline in net investment income year-over-year says otherwise.

So, the delayed impact of lower base rates coupled with a slowing economy seems to be now showing up in BDC financials. And investors going forward should proceed cautiously.

What BDC Investors Should Look For

For instance, BDC CION Investment Corp (CION) is a BDC I was high on with a buy rating last year. But since, their fundamentals have seemed to deteriorate, increasing their chances of a dividend cut.

CION has been plagued by one particular borrower, David's Bridal. They recognized losses of $22 million in the third quarter and saw their net asset value decline year-over-year as a result. They also saw declines in mark-to-market value in 8 companies total for 2024.

In their latest quarter, NAV declined further, down from $15.43 in Q4 to $14.28, driven by further mark-downs in portfolio companies.

One of those companies? David's Bridal and the other Anthem Sports. In comparison to stalwarts like SBUX & WMT, both are smaller, lesser-known companies that would likely suffer greatly in a recession.

CION's non-accruals stayed flat from the previous quarter at 3.2% at cost and 1.2% at fair value.

These were less than 1% the year prior. CION's financials have also deteriorated over the past year, a result of lower base rates impacting portfolio yields, and diminishing credit quality.

Net investment income declined from $0.60 or $32.6 million to their most recent $0.36, or $19.3 million, a drop of 40% on a per share basis. With the decline, this puts their dividend coverage at 100%, down from 176%, a significant decline.

This makes a dividend cut in the coming months likely. But, all BDCs are not created equal. For example, ones I think would do well and continue paying their current dividends are Ares Capital (ARCC) & Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL).

In ARCC's latest quarter ARCC saw their net investment income decline year-over-year. However, only slightly with a $0.01 drop from $0.55 to $0.54, giving them solid coverage of 113%.

BXSL's decline was more noticeable, down $0.04 from $0.87 to $0.83. As a result, dividend coverage declined from 113% to 108%. As mentioned previously, both saw their overall portfolio yields decline due to lower base rates.

ARCC's portfolio yield declined from 12.4% to 11% while BXSL declined from 11.8% to 10.2% due to floating-rate exposure. But, both companies are conservative and will likely continue paying their current dividends.

This is because of their investment-rated balance sheets and robust liquidity. ARCC's liquidity was $6.8 billion compared to $3.4 billion for the latter. While this doesn't guarantee dividend safety, it does give them some flexibility.

Both also have significantly larger borrower median EBITDAs. Ares Capital's was $274 million while Blackstone Secured Lending's was $210 million.

Another way I like to check a BDC's quality is NAV growth over time. This can be impacted by debt repayment, share issuance, and excess earnings in the short term. But over time, NAV should see solid growth.

As stated earlier, CION's NAV suffered a steep decline from the previous quarter. Year-over-year this was even higher, falling from $16.05, a double-digit decline of 11%. ARCC & BXSL both saw slight NAV growth annually.

Q1’25

Q1’24

CION

$14.28

$16.05

ARCC

$19.82

$19.53

BXSL

$27.39

$26.82

Moreover, both ARCC & BXSL trade at a premium above their NAVs, meaning they can issue shares for additional liquidity and this wouldn't negatively impact their NAV growth.

Their leverage was also below the sector's median's 1.19x. Ares Capital's was well-below at 0.98x. CION Investment's was above at 1.39x while Blackstone Secured Lending's was in-line at 1.19x.

Both ARCC & BXSL saw non-accruals stay steady year-over-year, another testament to their overall credit quality.

Ares Capital's non-accruals in their latest quarter were 1.5% at cost and 0.9% at fair value. These stood at 1.7% and 0.6% in the previous year's quarter. BXSL's were 0.3% at cost and 0.1% at fair value, a slight uptick from 0.1% at cost.

Risks & Bottom-Line

These 3 BDCs were chosen to show the vast comparison between smaller and larger BDCs as well as credit quality.

Although most BDCs would suffer in a recession due to their business models, investors should look to buy those with stable fundamentals.

Items like non-accruals, liquidity, size & scale, and spillover income are important items to look for when buying BDCs.

Moreover, investors should keep an eye out for further rises in non-accruals as well as reliance on payment-in-kind income.

Lastly, dividend coverage for all BDCs will likely become tight in the coming months as their portfolio yields will be lower as a result of lower base rates.

But as mentioned earlier, I do expect some to successfully navigate while others will likely see cuts to their dividends.

Furthermore, heightened uncertainty and recession fears will likely limit any significant upside and the sector could see underperformance going forward in terms of price appreciation.

However, look to take advantage of price dips, particularly in those with stronger fundamentals like Ares Capital, Blackstone Secured Lending, and other larger BDCs with stable or growing NAVs and strong balance sheets.

data.title: Scale Has Its Advantages: 3 A-Rated REIT Monopolies
data.description:
Adam Smith famously wrote that “the division of labor is limited by the extent of the market”.

In such, Smith believed that the dynamic force behind these powerful words is that with division of labor, markets should expand and become more productive.

Simply put, Smith was referring to the concept of scale, which is one of the most important competitive advantages (or economic moats) of an organization.

My first experience with scale was my first job, as a retail developer in which I assisted with building properties for many well-known brands including Advance Auto, Dollar General, PetSmart, Talbots, CVS, Walgreens, Blockbuster Video, Waffle House, Cato, Econo Lube, Aaron Rents, Tractor Supply, McDonald’s, Walmart, and others.

I witnessed firsthand how these companies expanded their footprints to attract more capital, improve network effects, and reach milestones, that in turn gave them strong competitive advantages over rivals.

Some of these companies (i.e., Dollar General (DG), Walmart (WMT), Tractor Supply (TSCO) and McDonald’s (MCD)) thrived because of scale, in addition to disciplined risk management practices, marketing, and customer service – all necessary ingredients for the perfect “wide moat” recipe.

Others failed (i.e., Blockbuster Video, Econ Lube n Tune) as a result of leverage, over expansion, technology, or poor management.

Several of my former tenants (i.e., Advance Auto and Walgreens) are recalibrating their business models in hopes of regaining their once dominant footing that was achieved over decades of expansion.

Having over three decades of experience as a developer has provided me with keen insight as it relates to scaling certain business models.

And these days, technology has become a huge disruptor that has allowed firms to utilize almost infinite data to research, diagnose, forecast, design and advertise products. These larger companies (with a scale advantage) can collect this data, which provides them with an extraordinarily wider moat.

I’ll be attending the ICSC Retail conference in a week or so in Las Vegas where I’ll meet dozens of thought-leaders in commercial real estate.

I’ve been attending this conference for over three decades, and it’s amazing to see the rapid growth in technology over the last decade or so.

Technology has streamlined the process for many retailers to make quicker site selection decisions, thereby reducing mistakes and expediting leasing and construction processes.

In addition, landlords can scale their platforms utilizing technology to reduce “the division of labor” and to enhance the overall quality of their customer base.

I’ve witnessed this evolution over three decades, especially from a landlord’s perspective.

While I thoroughly enjoyed my previous career as a small-town real estate developer, I would never want to start all over again (as a small developer).

The dominant REITs that I will tell you about below are simply unstoppable, and their moats are so large that it’s impossible to beat them, so instead I’ll join them (as a shareholder).

After all, scale has its advantages…

The Monthly Dividend Company®

Realty Income (O) is the largest net lease in the world, with a portfolio of 15,600 free-standing properties in all 50 U.S. states, the U.K., and six other countries in Europe.

Scale is one of the company’s primary competitive advantages and one of the reasons that the company has been able to generate impressive earnings and dividend growth over five decades (founded in 1969).

By diversifying (1598 different clients doing business in 91 industries) Realty Income has been able to support its ability to execute large transactions without creating outsized exposure to any particular client, industry, or geography.

For example, during COVID-19, when most gyms and theaters suspended rent payments, Realty Income was able to grow earnings (AFFO per share) and dividends.

Due to its scale advantage, throughout 2023 and 2024, Realty Income deployed $13.4B into investments, including seven unique transactions that made up nearly $6.8B, illustrated below:

As you can see, Realty Income has added new verticals to its platform, such as casinos and data centers, further expanding into these new verticals which has significantly increased the size of the company’s total addressable market.

Currently, the company’s portfolio occupancy is 98.5%, with a weighted average remaining lease term of approximately 9.1 years.

Most importantly, Realty Income has achieved above 100% recapture (of rents) at expiration, as the company has released over 6,000 properties at 103% recapture since 1996.

Incidentally, Realty Income is one of the few net lease companies that disclose re-leasing results.

In addition to scale, Realty Income also boasts a fortress balance sheet with single-A credit ratings from Moody’s (A3) and S&P Global (A-). I’ve been covering the company for over 15 years, and I can attest to the evolution of the balance sheet, which has allowed the company to expand in Europe.

In Q1-25, the company invested $893 million in Europe at an average initial cash yield of 7.0% (accounting for 65% of total investment volume). As CEO Sumit Roy explains:

“..our international presence and capabilities differentiate us from most other net lease platforms. Importantly, it offers us geographic diversification, which helps navigate country specific uncertainties. This expansion into Europe demonstrates how our entrance into new verticals can create immediate value and achieve scalability in a relatively short time.”

Roy also added that the company is “on track to deploy approximately $4 billion in investments throughout 2025.”

I would not rule out additional M&A or large-scale sale-leasebacks in 2025, given the history of such transactions.

Remember that in November 2021, the company merged with Vereit (formerly ARCP) , which provided attractive cost synergies (~$50 million) for the monthly dividend company. Then in January 2024, Realty Income acquired Sprit Realty in a $9.3 billion stock deal.

As you can see below, Realty Income was able to boost its earnings (AFFO per share) in 2021 and 2022 (9% each year) by utilizing its scale and cost of capital advantages. Since 2008, the company has generated CAGR of 5.3% (and 5.4% over the last 5 years).

Although growth estimates are below 5% in 2025 and 2026 (around 3% per year), I believe Realty could increase investment activity, which could in turn boost earnings and dividends.

Nonetheless, given the enhanced diversification attributes and sound payout ratio (75%) I fully expect to see Realty inch closer to “Dividend King” status. As shown below, the company has paid and increased dividends for 30 years in a row.

In terms of valuation, even though Realty has outperformed +6.1% both (VNQ) +.10% and (SPY) -3.7%, I find the shares attractive, trading at 13.5x (versus normal P/AFFO of 17.2x).

The dividend yield of 5.7% is also attractive, especially when compared to other “wide moat” stocks with similar scale and cost of capital advantages. My conservative forecast has Realty returning 16% annually (20% is most likely set up).

The Warehouse Robot

Prologis (PLD) is an industrial REIT that owns 5,900 properties (over 1.3 billion square feet) across four continents and 20 countries.

This includes 800 million square feet in the U.S. (86% of NOI), 129 million square feet in Other Americas (5% of NOI), 251 million square feet in Europe (8% of NOI), and 113 million square feet in Asia (1% of NOI).

This massive warehouse business has over 6,500 customers that generate over $6.3 billion of Net Operating Income.

In addition, Prologis is an active developer with over $4.5 billion in various stages of construction and lease up, as well as $41 billion of potential build-out with its land holdings. As seen below, the development margins are impressive, gross IRR’s of over 20%.

Prologis’ scale and customer-focused strategy have compelled the company to expand services, including 638MW Solar storage that generates 11-14% IRR.

In addition, Prologis has expanded into data centers via a 10GW long-term opportunity set.

A typical warehouse costs from $25 million to $50 million, whereas a data center can cost $150 million to $500 million. Margins are much better for data centers (15-20% for warehouses and 25-50% for data centers), which allows Prologis to generate superior returns.

Prologis has around 400 MW in advanced stages in a Tier 1 market and the company said that it’s having conversations with utilities and hyperscalers that have been very productive.

Similar to Realty Income, Prologis also has a “fortress” balance sheet that includes two single-A ratings from Moody’s (A2) and S&P Global (A). The company has impressive credit metrics that include 26% debt to gross market cap and 4.9x debt to adjusted-EBITDA.

As a result of this scale and cost of capital advantages, Prologis has generated robust earnings growth over the last five years, averaging 16% from 2020-2023.

Note that analysts expect Prologis to grow by 4% this year and 10% in 2026 and 2027.

Also (as shown below) Prologis was forced to cut its dividend during the GFC, however it has achieved steady growth over the last decade (averaging 10.8% CAGR).

Prologis has modestly outperformed (+1.1%) (SPY) +.10% and (VNQ) -3.7% year-to-date, and I find shares attractive trading at 24.9x (compared with the normal P/AFFO of 26.0x). The dividend yield is 3.8%, and I expect to see the distribution increase in line with the AFFO growth.

As seen below, I forecast Prologis to return 15% (conservative) to 20% (most likely) over the next 12 months as the company is “built to not only withstand market disruptions but to take advantage of opportunities as they arise” (from the CEO on Q1-25 earnings call).

The Orange Door Storage Juggernaut

Public Storage (PSA) is a self-storage REIT known for its iconic orange doors that can be seen at any of the company’s 3,399 properties in 40 states, representing 247 million square feet.

In addition, the company holds a 35% interest in 318 self-storage facilities (17 million net rentable square feet) located in seven countries in Western Europe under the Shurgard® name.

The competitive advantages unique to Public Storage, including a leading operating platform, multi-factor external growth strategy, and strong balance sheet, all are considered “wide moat” attributes essential to the durability of the business model.

As referenced earlier, a big part of the Public Storage “moat” is also technology, in which the company has developed and implemented a comprehensive and centralized digital ecosystem that connects all aspects of the business.

Customers (over 2 million) utilize the omni-channel digital tools to rent units, manage their accounts, and receive live customer care. In addition, PSA’s centralized security team monitors for safety through digital cameras and property access systems.

In 2019, self-selected digital options were 19% of customer interactions and currently it's over 85%.

Real-time data provides insight into how and when customers use PSA’s properties, and cross-platform cohesion has enabled a shift to optimized staffing based levels.

Like Realty Income and Prologis, Public Storage can also be described as a “fortress” in terms of its balance sheet. The self-storage REIT is the only U.S. REIT with A2 and A credit ratings from Moody’s and S&P, respectively.

With 3.9x net debt and preferred equity to EBITDA, retained cash flow is expected to increase from $400 million in 2024 to $600 million in 2025, and a strong operating profile, PSA has advantageous access to capital and significant capacity to fund future growth.

As seen below, PSA has grown AFFO per share by 7% CAGR since 2008 and by 10.3% over the last 5 years.

PSA’s dividend record has been lumpier than the previously referenced A-rated REITs. As you can see below, there were no dividend increases from 2017 to 2022; however, the company boosted the dividend by 50% in 2023. Since 2008, the dividend has grown by 10.1% CAGR.

PSA has modestly outperformed (+1.5%) (SPY) +.10% and (VNQ) -3.7% year-to-date, and I find shares attractive trading at 20.0x compared with the normal P/AFFO multiple of 22.1x.

The dividend yield of 3.9% is well-covered (76% payout ratio) and analysts forecast growth of 6% in 2025 and 4% in 2026. As shown below, I forecast shares to return around 15% over the next 12 months.

In Closing

While I find Realty Income, Prologis, and Public Storage to be 3 of the “widest moat” REITs, there are a few other honorable mentions worth mentioning; these include:

Welltower (WELL)

VICI Properties (VICI)

Mid-America (MAA)

Extra Space (EXR)

Digital Realty (DLR)

Regency Centers (REG)

American Tower (AMT)

As Warren Buffett explains, “the fundamental basis of above-average performance in the long run is sustainable competitive advantage”.

Over the last decade or so, technology has made it much more efficient for REITs to drive scale advantage, which allows for them to enhance operator and geographic diversification.

In the U.S., REITs own approximately 10% of all institutional-quality real estate, and these three REITs (referenced above) enjoy superior scale and cost of capital advantages, which should allow them to outperform over the long term.

In addition, we find the shares (of all three) attractive, which means that investors are positioned to generate optimized risk-adjusted returns.

“Economies of scale are a good thing. If we didn't have them, we'd still be living in tents and eating buffalo.” Jamie Dimon

data.title: Nokian Renkaat: A Double Or Quits Type Of Play
data.description:
Nokian Tyres (OTCPK:NKRKF) (OTCPK:NKRKY) is a Finnish manufacturer and retailer of tyres. Nokian’s strengths have been a strong brand and a high-quality and innovative product portfolio. In recent years, Nokian has gone through a shift of operational focus from east to west. Although the company appears to be the same on the surface, there are fundamental changes. After a steep decline in share price, we can try to understand what the company needs to achieve in order to an investor achieve decent returns.

A transformed company

In 2024, Nokian had a revenue of €1.3 billion and 3800 employees. Over half of its revenue comes from passenger car tyres and less than one fifth comes from heavy tyres. Geographically, Nordic countries represent half of the revenue. In addition to tire manufacturing, Nokian also runs a chain of tire shops called Vianor which represents approximately 30% of the annual revenue.

Nokian was a major loser of the Ukraine war. The company had a significant business, and it was a well recognized brand in Russia. Sales in Russia represented one fifth of its annual revenue and 80% of its passenger car tyres were manufactured in Russia. Furthermore, much of its competitive advantage was based on lower production cost supported by low labour and raw material costs in Russia.

Now the company needs to both establish new markets and competitive operations. It has invested in new production in the U.S., Finland and is ramping up production in Romania. The new factory in Romania is a €650 million investment and the first tyres were delivered in March. At the end of Q1, the company already had almost 4500 employees. The Romanian factory is about to reach its full capacity of six million tyres in 2027.

Nokian has thrived in the competitive industry by focusing on winter tyres and positioning itself in the premium tire segment, but it might not have the same brand image in the new markets it now needs to sell its tyres to.

Recent financial performance

In 2024 Nokian’s revenue grew by 10% and in Q4 Nokian’s revenue grew by nearly 13%. The earnings per share landed in negative territory, -€0.17. Recently, Nokian published its Q1 results. Its revenue developed positively and increased 14.7%. However, EBITDA margin continued to decline from 5.3% to 4.6% due to higher raw material and SG&A costs in the passenger car tyres segment.

According to the company, its revenue developed better than the market overall. In Q1 Nokian raised its prices to compensate for the increased raw material prices. The shares declined sharply against the negative profitability development. Further on, Nokian will place more effort on the profitability of the business and operations.

The situation still remains foggy

An investor buying into Nokian stock needs to be optimistic about the transformation of Nokian. Basically, it has shifted its attention from east to west, where it does not have the same brand recognition or sales channels in place, and building them takes many years. One of the key questions is, what kind of revenue and profitability level Nokian is able to achieve in the future and what is the right valuation for it.

In the old world Nokian was able to achieve a revenue level of €1.5-1.7 billion. Its gross profit margin was over 45% and the net profit margin fluctuated in a range of 15-25%. Nokian had very little debt or a significant net cash position. Now, its revenue is a couple of hundred million dollars lower and gross profit margin is below 20%. The lower revenue and margin is at least partly a result of the transformation, which has also resulted in a long-term debt burden of €740 million.

Some of Nokian’s performance level can be explained by constrained production capacity. The recent sales performance has been supported by better product availability. Profitability is still burdened by the transformation. Still in Q1, Nokian’s investments were significantly higher than depreciation, €52 million versus €35 million. The year before the same figures were €70 million and €30 million.

In 2024 one fifth of Nokian’s revenue came from North America. Nokian has a new factory in Daytona in the U.S. It produces all-weather and all-season tyres for cars and light trucks. Nokian produces locally 85% of the tyres sold in the U.S. Local manufacturers can benefit over importers which represent 60% of the U.S. market if tariffs to the Asian producers remain in place.

In Q1 its home market, Nordics, grew by 5.9%. Promisingly, Nokian’s revenue is rapidly increasing in the regions where it needs to achieve growth. However, the growth was achieved against a weak comparison period which was burdened by union strikes in the Finnish factory and harbours. On a positive note, the company has reorganized itself and the volumes are growing. Now, Nokian needs to showcase it can sell its capacity and turn the scale into profits.

Shares have upside if the company succeeds

Due to the transformation of the company it is particularly difficult to slap a correct price tag on Nokian. In the best years Nokian’s enterprise value was €4-5 billion and currently the enterprise value is approximately €1.7 billion.

Nokian’s European peers, Pirelli and Continental’s tire division, are reaching EBIT margins of 14-16%. In the past 10 years, both Pirelli and Continental have been valued at an EV/EBIT multiple of 8.5. Nokian’s goal is to reach a revenue of two billion euros and an EBIT margin level of 15% in the next five years. This means that Nokian is not reaching back for its glory days.

In order to see upside in Nokian shares, one has to believe significant revenue growth to continue, profitability to reach Nokian’s goals and a similar valuation to its peers. The upside, in a five-year timeframe, starts from the company reaching €1.8 billion in sales and its targeted level of profitability. Therefore, an investor needs to have a lot of confidence in the management and prospects of the company.

At the time of writing, the Nokian shares are trading at €6.2. Naturally, there would be further upside in the stock price if Nokian is able to pay down its debt. Let’s assume that Nokian’s total debt is at a level of its current long-term debt of €740 million. Then, we can see upside in the stock price starting from lower revenue and profitability than the company is aiming for.

Nokian’s financial targets are far ahead in the future and many uncertainties remain about the revenue growth and profitability. Net income will be under pressure due to increasing depreciation and interest expenses, which in turn will be a negative for the acceptable multiple. Therefore, it appears that there’s no hurry to purchase shares.

With a 10% discount rate, which could be low considering the risks, the shares would be a buy if an investor believes the company would grow to €1.8 billion in sales and reach 14% EBIT margin. To reach the revenue, Nokian would need to grow approximately 8% per year. Considering the time frame, the risk and reward is a tough balance here.

The dividend was cut to preserve cash

Nokian aims to distribute half of its earnings as a dividend and in recent years the company has paid the dividend semi-annually. In 2021-2023 the company paid an annualized dividend of €0.55 per share. For the fiscal 2024 Nokian was forced to cut the dividend down to €0.25 per share. The ex-dividend date was 8th of May.

Conclusion

Nokian used to be a company with a strong niche brand in selected geographies. Now, the company has transformed itself to the new reality, and it’s time for execution in the new markets of the U.S. and Central Europe, where winter tyres have less of a prominence. Although Nokian is struggling with profitability at the moment, it is still a consequence of the transformation. The following couple of years will show if the company can turn the scale into profits. At the same time, shares could easily double if the company succeeds in executing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Positive FCF May Come Sooner Than You Think
data.description:
Rocket Lab USA, Inc. (RKLB) has performed exceptionally well in the past 12 months, both financially and in the stock market. Up almost 400% since my previous bullish article published in June 2024, RKLB has handsomely rewarded investors who bet on the company's potential to successfully expand from small payload deployments to large-scale deployments. After digesting Q1 2025 earnings and other key recent developments, I am convinced that Rocket Lab still has a long runway to grow. After updating my earnings model, I am also convinced that Rocket Lab will inch toward a breakeven free cash flow point in 2026.

Q1 Earnings Recap

Rocket Lab reported $122.6 million in revenue for Q1 2025, a YoY increase of 32%. Revenue, however, declined 7% sequentially, which the management attributed to lower-priced Electron launches and program contribution changes in its satellite manufacturing business. The company completed 5 launches in the first quarter compared to 4 launches a year ago, which was one of the main reasons behind the stellar YoY revenue growth.

Exhibit 1: Rocket Lab revenue and gross margins summary

Rocket Lab ended Q1 with a total backlog of $1.067 billion, with expectations for 56% of this backlog to be recognized as revenue within 12 months. The backlog consists of $422.2 million for launch services and $644.8 million for space systems. Although space systems backlog growth has not been stellar, launch services backlog doubled YoY aided by strong demand for launches across all vehicles (Electron, Neutron, and HASTE).

In Q1, Rocket Lab saw operating expenditure increasing sequentially to $94.4 million (vs. $88.4 million in Q4 2024) on a GAAP basis despite a decrease in SG&A expenses as R&D expenses crept higher. This was always coming as the company is aggressively spending on development to prepare Neutron for launch later this year.

Rocket Lab ended the quarter with $517 million in cash and short-term investments after reporting $83 million in free cash outflows during the quarter.

For the second quarter, Rocket Lab expects revenue of $135 million at the midpoint of its guidance, which suggests Q2 is likely to be a record-setting quarter for the company.

Exhibit 2: Rocket Lab Q2 guidance

Moving from the earnings print, in the next few segments, I will discuss why I believe Rocket Lab is well-positioned to grow.

Expansion Into New End Markets

My bullish stance on Rocket Lab is centered around the company's strategic expansion into new end markets. When I say new end markets, I am thinking of new geographies as well as market segments.

Neutron, Rocket Lab's reusable vehicle designed to launch larger payloads and constellations, opens new doors for the company to grow as larger projects often carry higher profit margins. Neutron's first test flight is expected to be completed in the second half of this year. The substantial R&D costs incurred to build Neutron are already delivering promising results, with the company being selected as one of five launch providers by the U.S. Space Force to launch high-priority national security missions. As confirmed by the U.S. Space Force in late March, Rocket Lab has been added to the Phase 3 Lane 1 contract (a $5.6 billion program), which enables the company to bid for launch programs alongside Blue Origin, SpaceX, ULA, and Stoke Space. The U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory also partnered with Rocket Lab recently to use Neutron for a rocket cargo mission.

As evidenced by these recent developments, Neutron is already opening up new doors for Rocket Lab to diversify its launch portfolio and expand into new markets. This is the first step to catering to the large-scale demands of commercial and government customers, which includes potentially winning contracts for constellation launches in the future.

Then there is the geographic expansion that is finally taking shape.

The UK Ministry of Defence selected Rocket Lab for its GBP 1 billion Hypersonic Technologies & Capability Development Framework to develop advanced hypersonic capabilities. This is the first time HASTE has been made available to the UK. This also marks Rocket Lab's strategic expansion into Europe, which is expected to be a major player in the space economy in the long run.

The acquisition of Mynaric (which is in the works) helps the European expansion further with its German roots.

Rocket Lab Is On Pace To Enjoy Durable Competitive Advantages

When I screen for long-term investment opportunities, I look for companies that show the potential to enjoy durable competitive advantages. Rocket Lab fits this description.

Rocket Lab's Electron, designed to carry small payloads, is the most frequently launched small rocket annually, with more than 20 launches expected this year. In the first quarter, as noted earlier, the company completed 5 successful launches.

Exhibit 3: Q1 successful launches

Being the clear market leader in this space provides Rocket Lab with a distinct edge to win new projects, as customers in this sector often prioritize safety over any other variable. Successfully and safely launching dozens of Electron missions annually in the recent past has helped Rocket Lab emerge as the go-to launch solutions provider for small payloads on a global scale. The below table offers a comparison between Rocket Lab and some of its competitors.

Rocket name Operator Average annual launches in the recent past Notes
Electron Rocket Lab 10-15 Most frequently launched small rocket in the world
Ceres-1 Galactic Energy 3-6 Leading small payload launcher in China
Vega Arianespace 2-4 One of the leading small payload launchers in Europe
Long March 6/11 CASC 2-5 A leading small payload launcher in China
Pegasus Northrop Grumman less than 1 Launch services have been scrapped largely
LauncherOne Virgin Orbit 2 Highly infrequent launches

In addition to Rocket Lab's strengthening market position, I am encouraged by its vertical integration efforts which allow the company to cater to all aspects of the space economy. In the long run, the goal is to become a constellation owner. I believe competitive advantages stem from vertical integration as well since the company does not have to rely on many third-party solutions providers to launch their rockets, which also gives much-needed freedom of mind to its high-profile customers.

Positive Free Cash Flow On The Cards For 2026

For a small, fast-growing company, the first step toward profitability is turning free cash flow positive. For Q1, Rocket Lab reported negative free cash flow of $83 million on the back of an operating cash outflow of just over $54 million. To get a better understanding of the company's free cash flow profile, please refer to the below table I created based on the Q1 report.

Key metrics Q1 2025
Revenue (millions) $122.6
Gross profit (millions) $35.2
Less: R&D (millions) $55.1
Less SG&A (millions) $39.3
Plus: Stock-based compensation (millions) $19.2
Cash operating income (millions) -$40
Less: interest (millions) $9.6
Less: taxes (millions) $0.2
Changes in working capital (millions) ($22.8)
Operating cash flow (millions) -$54.2
Less: CapEx (millions) $28.7
Free cash flow (millions) -$82.9

Please note that I have included the most relevant and useful key metrics from yesterday's earnings print in the above table.

Moving on to projections, I have tried to be realistic yet conservative. I have used several key assumptions in my model, including Neutron completing a successful test flight later this year, backlog growth for Neutron after the test flight, and increased cost benefit realization from the reusability of Neutron.

Below are some of the other assumptions I used.

  1. CapEx related to Neutron to peak by the end of 2025 and start declining gradually in 2026. A risk to this premise is that failed launches will lead to major cost overruns.
  2. Continued growth of the high-margin components business.
  3. Successful integration of Mynaric.
  4. Market share uptakes in both launch services and system solutions.
  5. No capital raises.
  6. Gradual increase in average selling prices of Neutron launches, resulting in a boost to gross margins.

My earnings model extends through 2030, but for the purpose of this analysis, I will only share the model outputs for free cash flow through 2026 to drive home the point that Rocket Lab is well-positioned to see improved free cash flows sooner than some investors and analysts expect.

Metric Q2 2025 FY 2025 FY 2026
Revenue (millions) $135 $558 - $600 $1,000
Gross profit (millions) $42.5 $177 - $194 $375
Less: R&D $50 $195-$205 $135
Less: SG&A $47 $176-$186 $165
Plus: SBC $14 $61-$65 $55
Cash operating income (millions) -$39.50 -$133 to -$132 $130
Less: interest (millions) $3.1 $19 - $20 $13
Less: taxes (millions) $0.3 $1 -$2 $3
Changes in working capital (millions) ($22.8) ($28) - ($30) ($44)
Operating cash flow (millions) ($10) - ($47.5) ($160) - ($190) $70
Less: CapEx (millions) $30 - $32.5 $109 - $131 $70
Free cash flow (millions) ($40) - ($80) ($270) - ($320) $0

As illustrated above, I expect free cash flow to inch toward a breakeven level by 2026, setting up the stage for Rocket Lab to focus on GAAP profitability in the years to come. The strong competitive advantages enjoyed by Electron, the massive potential of Neutron, vertical integration efforts, and overall growth in the space economy are central to my expectations.

Takeaway

I believe Rocket Lab has a long growth runway ahead as it expands into new end markets and geographies while enjoying certain competitive advantages. The space economy is still in its infancy, and Rocket Lab is well-positioned to benefit from the stellar growth expected in this sector. With free cash flows expected sooner than I initially thought, I feel comfortable with my RKLB investment. I am, however, keeping a close eye on a few risks such as potential launch delays of the Neutron test flight, competitive threats, and the possibility of a slowdown in the realization of the Electron backlog which may force the company to tap into capital markets.

data.title: EOS: Market Volatility Creating Better Opportunities
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Big tech has been in a slump, though, off of the significant lows they did hit. One fund to provide exposure to this area of the market is the Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (NYSE:EOS). It isn't a pure-play tech fund, but it has a significant sleeve of tech sector exposure. That would be thanks to its benchmark index being the Russell 1000 Growth Index. On top of investing in a heavier tech-leaning portfolio, the fund also utilizes an options writing strategy on the individual underlying names. This is a bit different from most of the other Eaton Vance option writing funds, which utilize options writing on indexes.

Since our last update, the fund has performed rather poorly, which wasn't all that unexpected. We last covered it near the beginning of this year, and valuations were getting really stretched. To help compress those valuations, it just needed a trigger, and that trigger was the trade war started by initiating across-the-board blanket tariffs.

EOS still trades at a slight premium, but with the overall slump, it could be providing a much better opportunity to start adding more aggressively to this fund.

EOS Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.88
  • Discount/Premium: 0.85%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.30%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.09%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.104 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

EOS' primary investment objective is:

...to provide current income, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation.

To achieve this:

The fund invests in a portfolio of primarily large- and mid-cap securities that the investment advisor believes have above-average growth and financial strength and writes call options on individual securities to seek to generate current earnings from the option premium.

Performance - Premium Returns Quickly

With broader market volatility, EOS did briefly slip into discount territory once again. However, that was fairly short-lived, and the fund is back at a slight premium. Though a premium also isn't all that unusual for this fund for the last several years, which has pushed the fund's average discount to become quite shallow.

Another measurement of better-looking valuation is the 1-year z-score, which sat at almost 2.5 last time and has now come down to a more modest 0.88. That indicates that the fund is still slightly overvalued based on the 1-year average discount, but isn't too richly priced. It's a much more comfortable level to be adding to a position, at least moderately.

When looking at the fund's performance YTD put up against the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), we can see that the fund's total share price returns are quite similar. However, the fund's total NAV returns have held up slightly better, this is from active portfolio selection and the options writing strategy.

The fund's benchmark is the Russell 1000 Growth Index, which carries around 394 holdings. However, EOS carries only 50 holdings, so they do mirror the index's exposure through a sampling approach. That said, similar to most other market-cap weighted indexes such as the S&P 500 Index and Nasdaq 100, there are a few names that get massive weightings. It essentially makes those positions that are out of the top handful less impactful on the index's overall performance; therefore, EOS can mirror the overall results pretty closely with a much smaller number of holdings.

In terms of historical performance for EOS, the fund has lagged the longer-term annualized returns when put up against the Russell 1000 Growth Index. However, when put up against other Buy/Write indexes, the results are much more favorable toward EOS.

Call writing strategies often cap upside when markets are rallying. Over the last decade, mega-cap tech names have driven most of the market, and we've been pretty spoiled with significant upside. So these results aren't too unexpected when looking back at the last 5 and 10 years and seeing such underperformance.

For EOS, they do have some flexibility, though, when it comes to the overall call exposure they carry. As of the last update, they only had a very small portion of the portfolio overwritten. At 8%, this is the lowest level that I can ever recall seeing. Even further, they were writing the calls at 7.5% out-of-the-money on average. That provides for some significant participation in upside over the course of an average 21 days to expiration.

While these stats are from the end of March 31, 2025, right before volatility really kicked into high gear before recovering, it can help to explain why EOS hasn't given up much upside on the back of the recent recovery in the last weeks.

Distribution Remains Reasonable

EOS provides investors a monthly distribution, currently working out to a rate of 8.30%. Due to the slight premium, the fund's NAV rate comes out to 8.43%. The NAV rate can be particularly important for the Eaton Vance funds, as they had historically started to trim their payouts if it began climbing over the 10% level.

Eaton Vance has historically been a bit more unique in that they will adjust their payouts based on the current environment in an attempt to preserve and limit NAV erosion over time. That is unlike some other fund sponsors who will try to maintain their payouts even when NAV rates move to unrealistic levels, and then ultimately have to cut even more down the road.

With that said, EOS will require capital gains to fund its distribution. That isn't uncommon for equity-focused CEFs, but EOS's net investment income is actually negative—or essentially a net investment loss. That isn't that common for most equity-focused CEFs, but when you start getting into the more tech-focused funds, it actually isn't that uncommon. So I don't necessarily see that as a negative for EOS either.

Watching coverage will mean watching NAV over time, if it is staying flat or rising, it could be said that they are sufficiently covering the payout to investors.

Some of that coverage can come from not only the fund's underlying portfolio but also the fund's written options. In 2024, they actually posted a loss on their written options strategy—which can be the result of closing options out for a loss. The reason to do that would be to participate in further upside, so that isn't necessarily a negative either, as those realized losses can be offset by realized or unrealized gains.

For tax purposes, the fund listed mostly long-term capital gains. Though, there was a component of non-dividend distributions (aka return of capital). Several of the EV funds are focused specifically on making ROC distributions a larger portion of their payout. It doesn't mean EOS won't have ROC classifications, but they just don't have the specific tax-managed policy when it comes to their investing policy.

One of the funds that tends to be fairly heavy on ROC was Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV). Interestingly, last year, EOS showed a higher allocation to ROC-characterized distributions when compared to ETV.

Either way, LTCGs are still quite tax-favorable when compared to ordinary income, meaning that EOS can still be appropriate for a taxable account for some investors.

EOS's Portfolio

When looking at the current sector composition of the fund, we continue to see that information technology remains the largest sleeve—which is to be expected. It is only slightly lower in terms of percentage weighting when compared to the fund's index. This is also slightly lower than the 46.43% listed in our prior update; at that time, the index was also showing a slightly higher 48.76% weighting.

As tech has been hit particularly hard in this sell-off, and more so the Magnificent 7 names, this makes sense. The more value-oriented sectors held up relatively well; they certainly sold off, but not to the same degree. This was also only up through March 31, 2025—the real drop-off didn't happen until April. So what we are seeing is the pullback in those mega-cap tech names before we hit the largest part of the slump.

Similar to the sector weightings, seeing some slight weighting shifts, we see some slight weighting shifts in the fund's top ten holdings. Overall, the top ten account for a dominating 56.75% of the fund's allocation.

Again, we are only seeing the holding weights as of March 31, 2025, meaning we could actually expect that the weightings here slid a bit lower, even going through April. NVIDIA (NVDA) had previously been listed at a 12.25% weighting, so we already saw that tech favorite seeing a reduced allocation based on it sliding lower.

One name that was bucking the tech sell-off trend exhibited in the Mag 7 names over the last few months was Netflix (NFLX). Perhaps, it is no surprise then that we saw NFLX make its way into EOS's top ten holdings above.

Looking back at the N-PORT, for the period ended December 31, 2024, NFLX was sitting at around a 2.33% weighting in the fund. So it just took a bit of outperformance to lift that allocation, even if the fund managers weren't actively doing anything during this period. With a turnover rate of 18% for all of 2024, they aren't the most active either. Instead, their active management primarily comes in the form of the option writing strategy. Interestingly, NFLX was previously part of the FAANG names of old. That was Facebook (META), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Netflix and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL). In the last several years, a couple of name changes and shifting market performance led to the Mag 7 names, leaving NFLX out.

Conclusion

EOS has joined the tech slump, making it a more interesting position to consider allocating some investment dollars. That would be relative to where it was standing when we last updated the fund, though, it is off the lows as the overall market has already started to recover from the volatility seen in early April. At one point, EOS even dropped to a discount, highlighting the power of increased volatility to provide for added opportunities when it comes to investing in closed-end funds.
